title: Woman with a Hat
location: San Francisco Museum of Modern Art
description: The painting that launched Fauvism and scandalized the 1905 Salon d'Automne, this portrait of Matisse's wife Amélie uses seemingly arbitrary patches of vivid green, violet, and orange across her face and hat. The canvas is a manifesto of pure color freed from descriptive duty — the green shadow on her nose and red streak across her forehead declaring that color could express light and form without imitating nature.

title: The Joy of Life
description: Matisse's monumental pastoral depicts nude figures lounging, dancing, and making music in an Arcadian landscape of impossible colors — pink trees, yellow grass, lavender sky. The sinuous contours and flat, saturated color planes anticipate his later paper cut-outs. Picasso was so provoked by this painting that he responded with Les Demoiselles d'Avignon, making it arguably the most consequential canvas of the early twentieth century.

title: Harmony in Red
description: Originally painted as 'Harmony in Blue' and then repainted, this monumental canvas dissolves the distinction between wall and table into a single saturated red field animated by blue arabesque patterns. A window at the left reveals a green and yellow landscape, but the real subject is the sheer enveloping power of a single color. It remains one of the most radical demonstrations of color as spatial experience.

title: The Turning Road, L'Estaque
description: A winding road curves through a sun-drenched Provençal landscape rendered in bold, flat patches of unmixed pigment. Trees explode in vivid reds and oranges, the road glows with lavender and pink, and the sky is a mosaic of blue and green strokes. Small figures walk along the path, dwarfed by the sheer chromatic energy of the landscape around them. It is one of the largest and most ambitious Fauvist canvases ever painted.

title: Mountains at Collioure
location: National Gallery of Art, Washington, D.C.
description: Painted during the legendary summer Derain spent with Matisse at the Mediterranean fishing village of Collioure, this landscape dissolves the hills into bold patches of unmixed color — violet shadows, orange earth, turquoise sky. Each brushstroke is a separate declaration of pure pigment, creating a mosaic of color that vibrates with the blinding light of the Midi.

title: Goldfish
description: A glass bowl of bright orange goldfish sits at the center of a lush, densely patterned interior garden scene. The fish glow like embers against the surrounding greens, pinks, and purples of trailing plants and flowers. Matisse was fascinated by goldfish after seeing them in Morocco, finding in their languid circling a model for the contemplative calm he sought in his art. The painting is a masterclass in complementary color — the orange fish electrified by the surrounding greens.

title: Open Window, Collioure
location: National Gallery of Art, Washington, D.C.
description: One of the key paintings of the Fauvist revolution, this small canvas frames the view from Matisse's hotel room through an open window onto the harbor of Collioure. The window frame, walls, and flower pots are rendered in vivid pinks, greens, and violets, while the boats and water beyond shimmer with broken strokes of pure color. The painting is a doorway between the interior world of the artist and the blazing Mediterranean light outside.
